Internship Details: On week during the summer of 2025, I had the opportunity to intern with PJ Dick and Trumbull, two well-known construction management and contracting companies headquartered in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. The internship gave me hands-on exposure to how large-scale building projects are planned and executed.  I observed how project managers, engineers, and crews coordinate to keep construction sites safe and efficient.  I visited job sites, attended meetings, and learned about scheduling, budgeting, and teamwork in construction.  I developed problem-solving, communication, and organizational skills and relationship with different trades that will help me in school and future careers.  The internship showed me what it’s like to work in the construction industry and helped me think about possible career paths in construction.
Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I am the only child of two teachers, and from a young age I have always loved working with my hands and tinkering. During COVID, when schools shut down, I missed my 8th grade wood shop class. At the same time, my family was building a house, and my dad and I began experimenting with small projects using leftover scraps. We followed tutorials on YouTube, which led to building an American flag, and eventually an American flag concealment box. A local gun dealer purchased one to display in his store, and that moment sparked the creation of my woodworking business, 724woodworksbybrady (Facebook: Brady Woodworks). Since then, I have carried a passion for building and creating that continues to grow.
What interests me most about construction is the ability to take raw materials and turn them into something lasting and meaningful. This career empowers my future by giving me the skills to start my own business building custom homes. Beyond personal success, I see this path as a way to make an impact in my community—providing families with quality housing, creating jobs, and contributing to the growth of the industry. My background, combined with my skilled trades education, will allow me to merge craftsmanship with entrepreneurship, building a career rooted in service and innovation.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
After graduation, I envision building a career that blends the hands‑on craftsmanship I’ve developed since childhood with the entrepreneurial drive I discovered through my woodworking business, 724woodworksbybrady. Starting that business during COVID and turning leftover scraps into projects people wanted to buy, showed me how much I enjoy creating something meaningful and sharing it with others. Since then, I’ve expanded my skills by taking on custom work, making plaques for Range Resources to use for community and employee awards, and even selling my products at their employee Christmas craft fair. These experiences taught me how to work with customers, manage projects, and take pride in the quality of what I build.
Looking ahead, I want to take everything I’ve learned and apply it on a larger scale by owning a business that specializes in custom home construction, rental property management, and house flipping. I’m driven by the idea of taking raw materials, or even rundown properties, and turning them into something lasting, functional, and valuable. My goal is not only to build a successful future for myself, but also to make a positive impact in my community by providing quality housing and creating local jobs.
My education at Pennsylvania College of Technology will be the foundation that allows me to reach these goals. Learning how to build homes from the ground up will give me the technical confidence to manage job sites, lead teams, and deliver work that meets the highest standards. Exposure to multiple trades and the chance to learn from experienced professionals will prepare me for the wide range of challenges that come with running a construction‑based business.
This scholarship will support me by easing the financial burden of my education, allowing me to stay focused on developing my skills and gaining hands‑on experience. With this support, I can fully commit to mastering my craft and building the knowledge necessary to turn my long‑term vision into reality. It represents an investment in my future as a builder, entrepreneur, and community‑minded leader.
Additional Financial Details:
As the only child of two teachers, I grew up understanding the importance of hard work, education, and financial responsibility. My family has always supported my passion for building and entrepreneurship, but pursuing a skilled trades education comes with significant expenses that we must plan for carefully. I am committed to this path, yet the financial reality of tuition, tools, and living costs will have a major impact on my ability to complete my degree without taking on overwhelming debt.

For an associate degree in Building Construction at Pennsylvania College of Technology, I estimate my tuition to be around $34,000 over two years. I will also need to cover $3,000–$4,000 in college fees and $1,000–$2,000 for required construction tools and safety equipment. Living on campus adds additional costs, with housing estimated at $7,000–$9,000 per year and a meal plan costing $3,000–$4,000 per year. Altogether, the total cost of completing my degree while living on campus is likely to fall between $58,000 and $68,000.
These expenses are significant for my family, and support from scholarships would directly impact my ability to pursue the education I need to build a future in the construction industry.
Activities/Extracurriculars:
Throughout high school, I have been actively involved in clubs and organizations that have shaped my leadership, teamwork, and service skills. In Student Council, I’ve helped organize dances, teacher luncheons, pep rallies, and fundraisers, including raising $2,000 for the Washington County Food Bank. Attending leadership conferences has strengthened my organizational skills and ability to lead with purpose.
My involvement in Future Farmers of America (FFA) taught me responsibility and discipline through hands on projects and competitions such as the AG Mechanics CDE. Organizing chapter events and participating in community service showed me the importance of teamwork, leadership, and giving back. FFA also helped me build confidence through public speaking and taking on new challenges. These experiences came together when I earned the Pennsylvania FFA Keystone State Degree, the highest honor a member can receive at the state level.  Receiving this award recognized the dedication I put into my SAE, leadership roles, and service. FFA has shaped me into a more capable and motivated individual, and the lessons I learned continue to guide the way I approach opportunities in the trades.
As a varsity cross country and track athlete, I’ve learned perseverance, mental toughness, and time management. Training daily and competing in long races requires resilience, while encouraging teammates has taught me that even individual sports thrive on support.
Through McFriends, a club supporting students with disabilities, I’ve helped organize Olympic-style game days that promote inclusion and school spirit. This experience deepened my compassion and leadership while building meaningful connections.
Finally, as President of the Ski and Snowboard Club, I plan trips and manage finances, fostering responsibility and collaboration. Skiing has taught me patience and resilience, while leading peers has strengthened my ability to guide and encourage others.
Together, these experiences reflect my dedication to service, leadership, and personal growth.

Internship Details: I completed a construction pre-apprenticeship with Illinois Center of Innovation in Chicago, Illinois. The pre-apprenticeship was a total of 4 months from start to finish. During the program students learned basic safety, how to use tools, general knowledge of main trades, and career development. Upon completion of the program I obtained NCEER certification, OSHA-10, CPR certification and Flagger certification. HVAC/R stood out to me the most within the construction pre-apprenticeship. I knew I wanted to further my education and training in specifically HVAC/R. I am now currently enrolled in HVAC/R training with Environmental Technical Institute located in Willowbrook, Illinois.
Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
My background is somewhat nontraditional for the skilled trades, but it has played a major role in shaping my decision to pursue a career in HVAC. I earned a bachelor’s degree in Criminal Justice, which taught me discipline, responsibility, and the importance of problem-solving under pressure. While I valued that experience and what it contributed to my personal growth, I realized over time that I was most fulfilled when I was working with my hands and solving practical, real-world problems.

That realization became even clearer after completing a four-month pre-apprenticeship with the Illinois Center of Innovation. During that program, I was introduced to several different trades and gained a basic understanding of how they operate. Although the experience was broad, it confirmed that HVAC stood out to me the most. I was drawn to the technical challenge, the constant learning, and the fact that HVAC professionals provide an essential service that directly impacts people’s comfort and quality of life. It’s a skilled trade that demands both technical knowledge and hands-on troubleshooting, exactly the kind of work I’ve always been naturally inclined toward.
Another major motivation for me is a close family friend who owns an HVAC company. Seeing firsthand the stability, independence, and pride they take in their work has been a powerful influence. My long-term goal is to eventually work for their company, continue developing my skills in the field, take on greater responsibility, and one day grow into a position where I can take over the business. Entering this field is not just a career change for me; it is an opportunity to build something sustainable, meaningful, and lasting for my future.

I see HVAC as a career that empowers me to make a real impact in my community. Quality climate control is essential for safety, comfort, and health, and being able to provide that service is both valuable and rewarding. Whether it’s helping families stay safe in extreme weather or supporting local businesses with reliable systems, I want my work to directly improve people’s lives while contributing positively to the industry overall.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Looking ahead after graduation, I envision myself building a stable, long-term career in the HVAC industry where I can continuously grow my technical skills and take pride in providing essential services to my community. My goal is to gain real-world experience in the field, become a highly skilled and reliable technician, and eventually step into leadership and ownership opportunities within the industry.

A skilled trades education in HVAC will give me practical, hands-on training that directly translates to the workforce. This education empowers me to earn a living through problem-solving, technical knowledge, and hard work—skills that will always be in demand. It also allows me to build a career based on tangible results and customer trust, which is extremely important to me.

The Skillpointe Foundation's Skilled Trades Scholarship would play a major role in powering me toward these goals by easing the financial burden of my education and allowing me to focus fully on mastering my craft. Skilpointe Foundation's represents  reliability and professionalism—qualities I strive to bring to my work every day. With this support, I can invest in the tools, training, and certifications needed to succeed, accelerate my career growth, and position myself to make a lasting impact in the HVAC industry
Additional Financial Details: I am currently paying for my skilled trades education out of my own pocket while managing monthly tuition, transportation, and everyday living expenses. Choosing this path has required real sacrifices after already having a bachelors of arts in criminal justice, but I believe strongly in the value of hands-on work and building a stable, meaningful career in the HVAC/R. Financial support through this scholarship would ease some of the pressure I face and allow me to focus more fully on learning, developing my skills, and becoming the best tradesperson I can be.

Internship Details:
I have been an apprentice Instrumentation and Controls Technician with CMC Steel in Mesa for the past ten months. I work on advanced electrical and control systems in a steel mill—an environment that is physically demanding year-round and especially brutal during Arizona summers. Long hours in extreme heat, noise, and heavy industrial conditions are part of the job.

As one of only two women in the maintenance department, I’ve had to build both technical skill and thick skin. Working in a male-dominated field comes with pressure to constantly prove that I belong. There have been challenging moments, but they’ve strengthened my work ethic and confidence. I focus on mastering each process, asking questions, and showing up consistently, no matter how tough the conditions.

I’m committed to earning respect through competence, resilience, and perseverance, and to setting an example for other women who want to enter the trades.
Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I grew up believing that if you want something, you work for it. That mindset is what led me into the trades. I like working with my hands and figuring things out. Instrumentation and controls caught my attention because it’s not just physical work — it makes you think. You have to understand how systems connect, how signals move, and how one small issue can affect an entire operation.

What keeps me interested is the challenge. No two days are the same. One day I’m troubleshooting a sensor, the next I’m helping track down a control issue that’s affecting production. The work matters. When something goes down in a steel mill, it impacts safety and everyone on shift. Being part of keeping things running gives me a real sense of purpose.

Being a woman in this field is a big part of my “why.” There aren’t many of us, and that’s obvious every day. I’ve had to develop confidence, speak up for myself, and prove through my work that I belong. But I don’t just want to succeed for me. I want other women to see that this path is possible. If I can become a leader in this industry, I want to mentor, support, and encourage other women coming in behind me so they don’t feel alone.

This career gives me stability and independence, but it also gives me a platform. I want to help shift the culture in the trades so skill and work ethic are what define you — not gender
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
After graduation, I plan to continue working as an Instrumentation and Controls Technician and really grow into the trade. I want to get to the point where I’m confident handling complex troubleshooting on my own and eventually step into a lead role. I don’t just want to clock in and out — I want to be someone who knows their craft inside and out.

Long term, I see this career giving me stability and room to grow. There’s always something new to learn in this field, especially with automation and technology constantly changing. I plan to keep building my skills, earning certifications, and becoming someone people trust when things get tough.

This scholarship would make a real difference for me. It would ease some of the financial stress that comes with school and training so I can stay focused on improving and moving forward. Skilled trade education has already opened doors for me, and support like this helps keep that momentum going.

For me, this isn’t just about getting a job — it’s about building a solid future and proving that women belong in this industry. Every step forward puts me in a better position to lead by example and help the next woman coming into the trades feel like she belongs too.
Additional Financial Details:
The biggest challenge for me right now is the cost of living. Between rent, utilities, gas, food, and school expenses, it adds up quickly. To keep everything covered, I work three jobs while completing my apprenticeship and schooling. It’s a heavy schedule, and most weeks leave very little time for rest, family, or anything outside of work and school.

I’m committed to finishing my education and building this career, but balancing multiple jobs with training can be exhausting. There isn’t much room for vacation or even regular downtime. A scholarship would ease some of that pressure and allow me to reduce my workload, focus more on learning, and perform at my best in both school and on the job.

I’m willing to put in the work — I already am — but financial support would make it possible to move forward with more stability and less strain.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I was born and raised in Brooklyn, New York, to Haitian immigrant parents who instilled in me the values of hard work, faith, and perseverance. Although I was born in the United States, my first language was Haitian Creole, and I learned English while helping my parents navigate a new country and culture. As a first generation college student, I understood early that education was not just about personal success, but about creating opportunity and stability for my family.

My inspiration to pursue welding comes from my father, who worked as a welder for over twenty years. I admired his dedication, skill, and the pride he took in building structures that served a real purpose. When he passed away from cancer, it was one of the most difficult challenges my family and I have faced. In the midst of grief, I made the decision to continue the path he loved. Welding became more than a career choice it became a way to honor his legacy while building my own future.

I am drawn to hands on work because I find fulfillment in creating something tangible and lasting. Welding requires precision, discipline, and problem solving. It challenges me to think critically while working with my hands, transforming raw materials into structures that have strength and purpose. There is a deep sense of accomplishment in seeing a finished product and knowing that my skill and focus brought it to life.

Through balancing school, work, and family responsibilities, I have developed resilience and determination. These qualities align naturally with the demands of the skilled trades. Welding allows me to combine technical knowledge, creativity, and craftsmanship, and it provides a pathway to a stable and meaningful career. Most importantly, it allows me to carry forward my father’s legacy while forging my own.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Looking ahead, I envision a career where I combine my Welding Technology training with Mechanical Engineering to become a welding engineer, bridging the gap between hands on fabrication and technical design. I see myself working on innovative projects in infrastructure, manufacturing, and engineering, using both practical welding skills and engineering knowledge to create safer, stronger, and more efficient structures. By combining these disciplines, I hope to contribute to advancing industry standards while applying the precision, discipline, and problem solving skills I have developed through my education and lab experience.

Beyond my professional growth, I am committed to creating opportunities for others, particularly women entering skilled trades. I aspire to establish a nonprofit organization that provides mentorship, training, and resources for women who may lack access or representation in welding and engineering fields. I want to demonstrate that women can succeed in trades traditionally dominated by men and provide a pathway for them to gain confidence, technical skill, and financial independence.

This scholarship will play a critical role in helping me achieve these goals. Skilled trades education involves significant expenses, including tuition, tools, safety equipment, certifications, and materials. Receiving financial support will allow me to focus on mastering technical and engineering concepts, maintaining high academic performance, and completing certifications that are essential for career advancement. It will also provide the opportunity to dedicate more time to leadership roles, mentorship, and community engagement within my program.

After graduation, I envision a life defined by purpose, resilience, and contribution. I will honor my father’s legacy by excelling in the field he loved, support my family, and serve as a role model for other women pursuing technical careers. With this scholarship and my education, I will have the foundation to achieve professional excellence, create opportunities for others, and leave a lasting impact in the skilled trades and engineering industries.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
My professional journey has been shaped by two deeply meaningful roles: serving as a classroom teacher and currently working as a Certified Nursing Assistant (CNA). Both experiences have strengthened my commitment to helping others, but it is my work in healthcare that has ultimately inspired me to pursue my Licensed Practical Nurse (LPN) credential.

As a former teacher, I developed strong communication, leadership, and problem-solving skills. I learned how to remain calm under pressure, manage diverse personalities, and support individuals with different needs and learning styles. Teaching required patience, empathy, and the ability to think quickly qualities that are equally important in healthcare. While I valued the impact I made in education, I often felt drawn toward more direct, hands-on service where I could support individuals not only intellectually and emotionally, but physically as well.

Transitioning into my role as a CNA allowed me to experience that direct care connection. Working closely with patients each day assisting with daily living activities, monitoring vital signs, providing comfort, and offering emotional reassurance has been incredibly rewarding. I have seen firsthand how compassionate, attentive care can improve a patient’s dignity, recovery, and overall well-being. Being present during vulnerable moments has reinforced my desire to expand my clinical knowledge and skills so I can take on greater responsibility in patient care.

What draws me most to hands-on work is the immediate impact it allows me to make. I appreciate being actively involved in improving someone’s comfort, safety, and health outcomes. I enjoy the physical engagement of healthcare work, whether assisting with mobility, performing basic clinical tasks, or collaborating with nurses and other professionals. Hands-on work keeps me engaged, focused, and purposeful.

Pursuing my LPN is the next natural step in my professional growth. It will allow me to build upon my CNA experience, deepen my clinical expertise, and contribute more meaningfully to patient care. I am motivated by a genuine desire to serve, to continue learning, and to make a tangible difference in the lives of those I care for each day.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Looking ahead, I envision my life and career after graduating as a Licensed Practical Nurse (LPN) as both professionally fulfilling and personally transformative. Earning my LPN will open the door to a stable and meaningful career in healthcare, where I can directly impact the lives of patients and their families. I see myself working in a hospital, long-term care facility, or community health setting, providing compassionate, patient-centered care while continuing to grow in knowledge and skill. My goal is not only to secure employment but to become a nurse who is known for empathy, professionalism, and dedication to quality care.

In the long term, I plan to continue my education and pursue my Registered Nurse (RN) license. Becoming an LPN is an important first step in building a strong clinical foundation. With experience and continued education, I hope to take on leadership responsibilities, mentor new nurses, and advocate for patients in underserved communities. Healthcare is constantly evolving, and I am committed to lifelong learning so that I can provide the safest and most effective care possible.

This scholarship plays a critical role in helping me reach these goals. Financial support reduces the burden of tuition, books, and clinical expenses, allowing me to focus fully on my studies and clinical training. Instead of worrying about financial stress, I can dedicate my energy to mastering essential nursing skills, preparing for licensure exams, and gaining hands-on experience. This support also brings encouragement and validation, reminding me that others believe in my potential and are invested in my success.

My education will equip me with the clinical competence, ethical foundation, and communication skills necessary to serve diverse patient populations. It will also strengthen my confidence and resilience qualities essential in nursing. Ultimately, this scholarship and my LPN education are not just stepping stones toward a career; they are investments in a future where I can make a meaningful difference in the health and well-being of others.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I am a nontraditional student whose path to the skilled trades has been shaped by both challenge and determination. I inherited a genetic condition called Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (CMT). From the age of 15 through my late twenties, I worked without limitation. Around age 27, however, my condition began to manifest more visibly through drop foot and reduced hand dexterity. As a result, I began experiencing frequent falls and was prescribed ankle-foot orthotic (AFO) braces for both legs.
Despite these challenges, I continued working until age 35, when my physical limitations made it impossible to continue in the type of work I had always done. At that point, I was awarded Social Security disability benefits. While this marked a major transition in my life, it also allowed me the opportunity to be fully present at home and raise our three children.
Today, my circumstances have evolved. My oldest daughter is now able to help care for her younger siblings, which has given me the opportunity to return to school and invest in my future. I am pursuing training in a skilled trade such as Computer-Aided Design and Drafting (CADD), a field that allows me to apply problem-solving, precision, and technical skills without being limited by my physical condition.
My goal is to return to the workforce full-time, regain financial independence, and no longer rely on SSI. I am driven by a strong work ethic, a desire to provide for my family, and the determination to adapt, learn, and contribute meaningfully through the skilled trades.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Looking ahead after graduation, I envision a return to the workforce in a stable, long-term career where my skills and reliability matter more than physical limitations. Over the past several months, employers have visited my school repeatedly and emphasized the strong demand for skilled drafters. Knowing that there is real work waiting for me gives me confidence and motivation as I prepare to graduate.
Immediately after graduation, I plan to apply to the employers I have already met and pursue any opportunity that allows me to gain experience and prove my value. I do not have a minimum salary requirement because I understand that I am entering this field at the beginning level. Just as I did in my twenties, I am prepared to start at the bottom, work hard, impress my employers, and earn advancement through dedication, reliability, and consistent performance.
My skilled trades education is equipping me with practical, job-ready drafting skills that directly align with industry needs. This scholarship will play a critical role in helping me reach these goals by allowing me to graduate debt-free. Without the burden of student loan payments, I will have the flexibility to accept entry-level positions based on long-term opportunity rather than immediate pay. This financial freedom will allow me to focus on building experience, supporting my family, and progressing toward full-time employment and independence.
Ultimately, this education and scholarship together empower me to re-enter the workforce with confidence, rebuild my career, and create a sustainable future for my family through the skilled trades.
Additional Financial Details:
As a nontraditional student, my ability to pursue skilled trades education is shaped by both personal and financial realities. I live with a progressive genetic condition, Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, which limits the type of physical labor I can perform. While this condition forced me to leave my previous line of work, it also led me toward a trade drafting that allows me to contribute meaningfully and productively without being limited by my disability.
Financially, I am supporting a family while transitioning from Social Security benefits back into the workforce. Returning to school represents a significant commitment and risk, as I am working toward long-term independence while managing present-day household responsibilities. Because of these circumstances, affordability is a critical factor in my ability to complete my education.
At the same time, my situation reflects strong motivation rather than limitation. I have a proven work ethic, years of real-world experience, and a clear plan to re-enter the workforce immediately after graduation. I am committed to starting at the entry level, gaining experience, and working my way up through dedication and performance.
Support such as the DeWalt Trades Scholarship directly impacts my ability to stay enrolled, graduate, and accept early-career opportunities that lead to long-term stability. Reducing the financial burden of education allows me to focus on learning, supporting my family, and building a sustainable career in the skilled trades. This opportunity would not only help me complete my education, but also help me fully transition back to independence and self-sufficiency.
Activities/Extracurriculars:
I volunteer with Westmoreland County Veterans for the War on Terror (WCVWOT), where I assist with distributing flags on Memorial Day and help local VFW posts decorate veterans’ grave sites with flags. Our organization also raises funds to create a permanent War Memorial at Twin Lakes Park, honoring those who have served.
I also volunteer with The At Ease Inn, where I serve as a primary contact person and manage equipment and furniture donations. These donated items are repurposed or sold, with proceeds reinvested directly back into the local community to support veterans and families in need.
Prior to returning to school full-time, I volunteered at a local elementary school an average of three times per week. My responsibilities included assisting in the school office with paperwork, reading to preschool and kindergarten students in the classroom, and supporting the school play by operating audiovisual equipment.
In addition, I volunteer with a local Boy Scout troop, helping run meetings and participating in community events such as Veterans Day ceremonies, parades, and camping trips. Through this involvement, I support youth development, leadership, and community service.
These volunteer experiences reflect my commitment to service, responsibility, and giving back values I carry with me into my education and future career in the skilled trades.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
My background is in engineering. I enjoyed disassembling things to see how they worked and then putting them back together. I was involved with many hands-on projects throughout my school years designing, fabricating, and assembling. The process of bringing an idea to life and then iterating through the first design to the final design was always interesting to me. 

However, as I pursued engineering, although still involved in project teams, I found that computer software had become more and more utilized. We used computers to design, fabricate, and iterate. CAE programs could take a design and eventually find a design solution to meet all given parameters. I moved further and further away from hands-on work and more to making assessments of "black-box" systems that essentially did everything for me. The engineering was more to do with ensuring that the result was physical and understanding what to do next. I was a glorified input mechanism for computer software designed by genius individuals. 

I found myself starved of actually possessing a real talent. So I decided to pick up a hobby of working on my car. It would save me some money and there was enough work to be done that I could maybe learn a thing or two and get back to what I originally enjoyed doing as a kid - figuring it out. I started simple and got more complex.

I had not grown up in a family with any handymen or wrenchers. Everything I learned from working on my car was self-taught with the help of manuals and some Youtube videos. As it happened, I was sitting in a Starbucks in a new state with no idea what to do next with my life and for 3 hours I sat thinking about what I wanted. After some research I came across aviation maintenance. An A&P certificate would take two years to acquire; I would learn all the basic skills I needed to succeed; and, it was a skilled trade where I could work with my hands. It was perfect. So, I started pursuing my A&P certificate this year.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
My vision for the future is pretty clear to me. I want to eventually blend my engineering background with my A&P license. The aviation industry has changed over the last few years. Field approvals have declined. Now, virtually all major repairs and alterations require engineering data to support. I think that is where I can bring something unique. I can be an A&P mechanic that can also do the engineering analysis and provide the engineering data for a repair station.

This goal will take time. There is a lot to learn about aviation maintenance. I want to first work in the industry for several years, gaining a lot of experience from seasoned veterans. I would like to experience the general aviation side as well as the private jet or commercial airliner side too. I want to be well-rounded in order to ultimately set up my own shop one day. I am new to the aviation industry but I am prepared to stay for the rest of my career. Airplanes are incredible and mechanics will always be needed to maintain and repair them.

This scholarship will help me do that. Admittedly, I am not attending a program with an insane tuition price tag. But, I am on my own. I dropped everything to pursue my A&P certificate. So any financial support helps. I take my schooling seriously; I study a lot. I aced my general written exam and expect to do the same for my airframe written and oral/practical exams. This scholarship will help me do so if I do not need to spend my time working a lot of part time hours to support myself for the basics.

The club raises money to provide support for those pursuing A&P certificates who also have an interest in learning to fly. Our club is involved in the community here at Sidney, attending events like the Fall Parade, cooking food at the car show, outreach at the Winter Festival, etc.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
About a year and a half ago, I joined a men’s group through my church. Together we read *Wild at Heart* by John Eldredge, a book that explores what it means to have the heart of a man and to seek adventure. Then in January 2025, my father passed away after a battle with cancer suddenly took a turn for the worse. Facing that profound loss gave me a chance to re-evaluate the way I viewed myself—as a man, a husband, and a father to my two-year-old son.

     During this period, my wife and I had already been discussing the future of my career. I worked in fundraising for an organization I believed in, yet I felt unfulfilled. I had a deep appreciation for the mission, but didn’t feel the direct impact I hoped to make. I realized I needed a career reset—something that would allow me to work with my hands, build tangible skills, and eventually pass those skills on to my son. With this in mind, I left my fundraising job this past June and began searching for a new direction—an adventure much like the one described in “Wild at Heart”.

     Aviation has fascinated me since childhood. When I was young, my mother would take me to get donuts and watch airplanes take off at the San Francisco airport, memories that stayed with me. I considered becoming a pilot, but I knew that lifestyle wouldn’t be practical for my family. Learning about the growing demand for aircraft mechanics, something clicked. Aviation maintenance offered everything I wanted: meaningful work that I was excited about, the challenge of learning a new skill that would allow me to work with my hands, and an industry that truly needs dedicated workers.

     Around this time, I also reached out to a friend I had met through my previous job—a leadership consultant who works closely with CEOs and specializes in the Birkman Method. After taking the assessment, I learned that I have a strong mechanical aptitude—something I hadn’t recognized in myself. This discovery gave me confidence that I was moving toward the right career.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
Growing up, agriculture was not just something my family did, it was a way of life.  Some of my earliest memories are of being on the farm with my grandpa and my dad.  My grandpa farmed for many years, and now my dad continues that farming operation.   Because of them, I didn’t just grow up around tractors and equipment — I grew up loving them!

As a baby, I was riding in tractors, in my bucket car seat.   My parents would joke that before I could even walk, I was in the cab.  The sound of a diesel engine running  would put me right to sleep in the tractor.   Machinery and  smell of fresh dirt became normal parts of my childhood.  As I got older, I wasn’t just riding along — I was playing with toy tractors, lining them up in the living room, grass, sandbox pretending I was planting or harvesting right alongside my dad.  My favorite toys were always the realistic farm equipment models. Big or small, the small ones fit in my pockets and went with everywhere.   While other kids played with cars and dolls, I dreamed about big machines and open fields.

Choosing to become an agricultural diesel technician feels natural to me.  It combines the world I grew up in with hands-on mechanical skill.  I appreciate how important diesel technicians are to farmers.  We help families like mine continue their operations.  There is pride in knowing that when a tractor or combine starts running again, you played a part in keeping the farm productive.  I loved the feeling of leaving my first  service call as an apprentice and how happy they were to get up and running again.

This career isn’t just a job to me — it’s a continuation of my family’s agricultural legacy.  From riding in tractors as a baby to working along side my dad, grandpa and uncles, to becoming an agricultural diesel technician allows me to stay connected to my roots while building a strong future.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship: When I graduate, I plan to pay it  forward by supporting new apprentices and students entering the diesel technician field.  I want to be the kind of technician who helps others feel capable, welcomed, and supported, just like the people who helped me.  I want to answer questions without judgment, take the time to explain things clearly, and help new students build confidence as they learn.  I hope to encourage more young people to consider diesel technology as a rewarding career path, especially those who might not feel drawn to traditional college routes but enjoy working with their hands and problem solving.
Additional Financial Details:
I have been fortunate not to face many major obstacles in my life.  I come from a supportive family who encourages me to follow my goals and work hard toward them.  
My parents always say it’s a “farmer thing” when you grow up on a farm, you learn early that you can’t let circumstances  affect you too far out of the norm.  No matter what is happening, the animals still need to be fed, the chores still need to be done, and life keeps moving forward.  That mindset taught me to stay steady, responsible, and focused even when things get tough.

However, one challenge I am currently facing is the financial burden of attending college out of state. The school I hope to attend is highly recommended for the Diesel Technology degree I want to pursue, but going out of state comes with higher costs than a local technical college.

There is no on campus housing, which means I will need to relocate, find a place to live, and secure a new job while balancing my studies.  The upfront expenses for tuition, tools, and materials are also significant.  My parents are not able to help much financially, so I will be responsible for paying most of the costs on my own.

Despite these challenges, I am determined to succeed.  I plan to work hard and save as much as possible during the summer and continue applying for scholarships throughout my college years. I believe that with dedication and hard work, I can overcome these financial obstacles and achieve my goal of becoming a skilled Diesel Technician.
Activities/Extracurriculars:
From second grade through my sophomore year of high school, I played basketball, learning the value of teamwork, discipline, and determination. During my junior year, I participated in a youth apprenticeship working as a diesel technician apprentice. I continued working full time during the summer months and committed to a second year of the apprenticeship during my senior year. These experiences taught me responsibility, time management, and the importance of dedication.   During this time I found a love for and drive wanting to become a certified diesel technician 

I am also active in my community, volunteering each year at my church’s spaghetti dinner, helping prepare the meatballs, and participating in our annual spring yard cleanup. I take pride in having a strong work ethic and truly enjoy helping others.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
Starting welding in high school changed the direction of my life in ways I never expected. At first, I signed up for the class because I thought it would be cool to learn how to use tools and work with my hands. I didn’t realize it would open the door to real opportunities and a career that could make serious money. As I learned more about the trade, I discovered that skilled welders are always in demand. From pipelines to skyscrapers to custom fabrication, welding is everywhere. Knowing that I could graduate with a skill that companies are willing to pay well for gave me confidence about my future.

Growing up, I always wanted better for my kids than what I had. I didn’t want to struggle financially or live paycheck to paycheck. Welding showed me that I don’t have to follow a traditional four-year college path to be successful. With certifications, hard work, and experience, welders can earn strong incomes and even start their own businesses. That possibility motivated me to take every project seriously and push myself to improve.

Over time, I fell in love with the trade. There’s something satisfying about striking an arc, laying a clean bead, and watching two pieces of metal become one. Welding requires patience, discipline, and pride in your work. The more I practiced, the more I wanted to be the best I could possibly be. I began thinking about competitions, advanced certifications, and earning a scholarship to continue my training after high school.

Welding is more than just a class to me now. It’s a pathway to financial stability, a better future for my family, and a career I genuinely enjoy. Starting in high school gave me a head start, and I’m determined to turn that opportunity into success.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
don’t just want to be a welder — I want to be one of the best. Ever since I started learning, I’ve taken pride in every bead I lay and every project I finish. There’s something about welding that feels bigger than just a job to me. It’s a skill that takes time, patience, and a lot of effort to master. If I’m going to make a career out of this, especially working in pipelines or refineries, I know I have to hold myself to a higher standard. Those environments aren’t easy. This scholarship would help me get the tools and certifications I need to keep pushing myself. It’s not just about equipment — it’s about investing in my future. With the right support, I can keep growing, sharpening my skills, and becoming the welder I know I’m capable of being.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I am a lifelong resident of Germantown, Maryland, in Montgomery County, where my family has deep roots. We also have connections to Swayzee, Indiana, a small town with a strong work ethic. Raising my two young children here has been my top priority and has guided every major decision in my life. After high school, I focused on family and steady work, but I always felt drawn to practical skills, fixing things, and seeing real results from my efforts. Watching homes and neighborhoods change around us sparked my interest in building and maintaining safe, lasting spaces for families like mine.
This led me to pursue the Building Trades Technology program at Montgomery College. I have always enjoyed working with my hands, whether helping with home repairs or figuring out how structures fit together. Skilled trades like carpentry, electrical wiring, HVAC, or plumbing appeal to me because they are essential, in demand, and let me create something tangible. I love the problem-solving: diagnosing issues, finding the right fix, and completing work that improves people’s lives and stands the test of time. There is deep satisfaction in framing a wall, wiring safe systems, or installing efficient heating and cooling, knowing the result provides real value and supports my family through meaningful, well-paid work.
This path has not been easy. Balancing parenting, household duties, and returning to school as an adult has demanded discipline and sacrifice. Financial pressures and family needs sometimes made it tough to commit, but I have pushed through by staying focused on the goal: earning certifications, entering an apprenticeship, and building a secure career. The hands-on labs at the Gudelsky Institute excite me most, working with real tools and materials in ways that match how I learn best.
Pursuing this trade is about creating stability for my children, contributing to our community, and finding fulfillment in work that combines effort, skill, and impact. I am eager to study at Montgomery College and apply these skills to build a brighter future, one project at a time.

As a lifelong resident raising two children locally, I see this as a way to contribute to the community while building financial security for my family. Through the Registered Apprenticeship-College Consortium, I intend to enter a paid apprenticeship in carpentry or a related trade after graduation. This will provide real-world experience, income while learning, and the required years toward Maryland’s Home Improvement Commission (MHIC) contractor license, which is necessary to legally run my business and offer services like decks and home fixes.
The scholarship eases financial pressures, allowing me to concentrate fully on classes, labs, and certifications without added strain from family responsibilities. Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I come from a white collar management background, where leadership, accountability, and operational performance shaped my daily work. I have spent years managing teams, solving complex operational problems, and ensuring results, but I realized I was drawn to work that is tangible, technical, and skill driven. I am still actively working in management while training and learning in the skilled trades, intentionally building hands-on expertise alongside theoretical knowledge. I am not limiting myself to one area of the industry, but I am currently focusing on welding because it provides a strong technical foundation that connects directly to both maintenance and fabrication.

What inspires me is the combination of technical challenge and practical application, the “why” behind every process as much as the “how.” I am fascinated by understanding the science behind materials, the structural reasoning that ensures strength and safety, and the engineering principles that govern performance and durability. This focus on theory as well as practice drives my passion for the trades, because it allows me to master skills in a meaningful, informed way rather than simply performing tasks. Hands-on work appeals to me because it provides immediate feedback, tangible results, and the opportunity to continually test and refine my knowledge. It is the intersection of thinking and doing that excites me and keeps me engaged.

Alongside my professional growth, I am a single father raising my fourteen-year-old child. Balancing work, parenting, and education has strengthened my discipline, time management, and sense of responsibility. I hope to eventually combine the skills and experience I gain in the field with formal teaching credentials to educate the next generation of skilled trades professionals. I want to share not only techniques, but also the deeper understanding of why processes work and how to apply them effectively.

Ultimately, I am pursuing the skilled trades because I want a career that challenges me intellectually and technically, provides opportunities for growth, and allows me to build a meaningful, hands-on future for myself and my family while inspiring others to do the same.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Looking ahead, I envision a career where I combine hands-on technical expertise with teaching and mentorship. After graduation, my immediate goal is to gain extensive real-world experience in the skilled trades, building the technical proficiency and credibility necessary to excel in the field. I want to understand the industry from the ground up, mastering not only practical skills but also the theory and reasoning behind every process. This foundation will allow me to pursue the credentials required to teach at the high school or technical college level, sharing both the “how” and the “why” of skilled trades with students who are just beginning their careers.

My long-term vision is to become an instructor who can bridge the gap between classroom learning and real-world application. I hope to inspire students to value both the technical mastery and the theoretical knowledge that drive quality and innovation in the trades. Teaching would allow me to pass on the skills, critical thinking, and problem-solving strategies I have gained, helping the next generation of tradespeople succeed while fostering a culture of curiosity, discipline, and excellence.

This scholarship and my education at Harper will be essential to achieving these goals. The financial support will allow me to focus on developing both technical skills and theoretical understanding without the added stress of balancing full-time work, parenting responsibilities, and tuition costs. The structured education and hands-on experience provided by my program will prepare me to enter the workforce confidently, gain the experience necessary for teaching credentials, and ultimately make a meaningful impact as an educator. By supporting me now, this scholarship helps lay the groundwork for a future where I can contribute to the trades not only as a skilled professional but as a mentor and teacher, shaping both my career and the opportunities of others.
Additional Financial Details: Although I was fortunate to receive a $2,000 scholarship in the fall, the cost of college remains a significant financial challenge. Tuition, fees, books, and required tools add up quickly, and as a single parent solely responsible for supporting my fourteen-year-old child, I must cover all household and educational expenses on my own. Balancing full-time work, parenting, and school requires careful planning and sacrifice, and every bit of financial assistance helps me stay focused on completing my education and building a career in the skilled trades. This scholarship would directly reduce financial stress and allow me to dedicate more time and energy to mastering both the technical and theoretical skills needed to succeed.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I grew up on an island where the horizon was never empty. The flight path ran directly over my childhood home, the light whine of inter-island turboprops banking toward the runway was the soundtrack of my childhood. I learned to tell aircraft apart by sound alone.

On that small strip of land surrounded by endless water, airplanes were more than machines. They were proof that there was a wider world. From the beach I could watch flights descending toward the airport, imagining where they had come from and who they carried.

Life, however, has a way of charting indirect routes. I left the island in his teens, not for the cockpit or hangar, but for steady work that paid bills and raised a family. Years turned into decades. Responsibilities grew heavier; dreams grew quieter. Still, never too far that first love. The old instinct never left me.

Now in middle age, with salt beginning to claim my hair, I returned to that early fascination—not as a wistful memory, but as a plan. Pursuing a license in aviation maintenance, trading comfort for classrooms and weekends for study sessions. I sit among younger students who grew up in a digital world that I had to learn later in life. Yet when discussions turn to engines, airframes, and safety systems, my eyes carry the same spark as the boy on the beach.

To me, aviation maintenance is not merely technical training. It is stewardship. Every bolt torqued to specification, every inspection signed off, represents a life carried safely across oceans. I understand, perhaps more deeply than most, what flight means to families separated by water and distance. I remember being the child who waved at passing aircraft, imagining unseen connections.

There is humility in starting over at this age, but there is also courage. The island taught me that horizons are meant to be crossed. The flight path that once thundered over my rooftop now guides me forward in a different way—no longer as distant spectacle, but a calling!
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Returning to school full time in the middle of my life is both an exciting and humbling decision. Pursuing my aviation maintenance degree means stepping away from steady, full-time income to focus on classes, labs, and hands-on training. While I am fully committed to this path, the financial strain of balancing tuition and everyday living expenses is very real.

Scholarship money would allow me to stay focused on my education without the constant worry of how to cover basic bills. It would help pay for rent, utilities, groceries, and transportation to and from campus. Instead of working extra hours to make ends meet, I would be able to dedicate that time to studying aircraft systems, completing projects, and preparing for certification exams.

As a full-time student, my schedule is demanding. Aviation maintenance is not just textbook learning; it requires long hours in the hangar and careful attention to detail. Financial support would ease the pressure of choosing between earning income and investing time in mastering the skills that will define my future career.

This scholarship would not be a luxury—it would be stability. It would provide breathing room so I can concentrate fully on becoming a safe, skilled, and dependable aviation maintenance technician. With fewer financial distractions, I can maintain strong academic performance and complete my program on time.

Receiving this support would mean more than help with bills. It would represent an investment in my commitment to finish what I started and to build a second career rooted in passion and purpose.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
One of my favorite shows growing up was This Old House. My fascination with how homes were built, repaired, and brought back to life should have been an early sign that construction would eventually become my career path. Although I initially pursued a different direction and earned a bachelor’s degree in Environmental Science, my interests continued pulling me toward a more hands-on field. Now, at 26, I have returned to school to pursue a Building Inspection Certificate and redirect my professional future.

Growing up in Phoenix, Arizona, where rapid development continually reshapes the city, strengthened my desire to be part of an industry that directly impacts communities. In 2024, I entered the safety field and quickly realized how essential detail-oriented professionals are to protecting workers and ensuring projects are completed with integrity. That experience inspired me to pursue building inspection, where I can combine my safety background with technical construction knowledge.

What draws me most to building inspection is the opportunity to serve as a bridge between safety teams, construction crews, and regulatory standards. I have seen firsthand how proper procedures and attention to detail prevent injuries and protect lives. Building codes exist because of lessons learned through past failures, and I believe we have a responsibility to uphold them so workers and future occupants can trust that structures are built safely and correctly.

This past year has reinforced my commitment to this path. As a newlywed balancing full-time work, finances, and education, my wife and I have faced unexpected challenges, including postponing major life plans and overcoming financial setbacks.

By becoming a building inspector, I hope to protect workers, improve project quality, and help build safer communities across Arizona while serving as an example for women entering the skilled trades. With this scholarship, I can complete my program by Fall 2026 and confidently build the future I envision for myself and my family.

My background in occupational health and safety has strengthened my skills in problem-solving, risk management, and attention to detail, which I plan to combine with the technical expertise gained through my Building Inspection Certificate. This path will allow me to contribute to the safety, quality, and integrity of projects across Arizona while building a professional reputation grounded in accountability and trust. As a woman entering a traditionally male-dominated field, I believe my work and dedication will speak for themselves as I establish myself as a respected professional.

I am particularly passionate about residential development inspections because of the challenges my generation faces when purchasing a home. By serving as a bridge between field crews, safety teams, and regulatory standards, I hope to contribute to safer workplaces and stronger communities while supporting projects that will serve families for generations.

This Home Depot scholarship will play a pivotal role in helping me achieve these goals. My background is in robotic engineering and computer science, where I developed strong technical discipline, systems thinking, and problem-solving skills. From an early age, however, my deepest interest has been aviation. As a child, I was captivated by aircraft and dreamed of one day flying my own airplane and contributing to the development of safer, more advanced aircraft. Aviation maintenance represents the highest standards of technical skill and safety. The responsibility of ensuring an aircraft is airworthy aligns with my commitment to detail, discipline, and continuous improvement. I am drawn to work where accuracy matters, procedures must be followed without compromise, and technical decisions carry real consequences. Maintaining aircraft is not just mechanical work; it is a critical role that protects lives and supports the broader transportation and economic infrastructure.

Transitioning from a technology-focused academic background into a skilled trade has required adaptability and perseverance. I have balanced rigorous coursework, financial constraints, and the challenge of mastering new mechanical competencies. Rather than viewing this shift as a change in direction, I see it as a strategic step toward becoming a more complete aviation professional, combining advanced technical knowledge with practical, workforce-ready skills.

Through hands-on training and industry-recognized credentials, I am preparing for a long-term career in aviation maintenance. My immediate objective is to earn Inspection Authorization (IA) within three years. Achieving IA represents more than a credential, it demonstrates mastery, ethical responsibility, and the capacity to uphold rigorous airworthiness standards.

I am driven by the weight of accountability inherent in maintenance work. Every task carries direct implications for the safety of pilots, passengers, and the public. I approach each task with the mindset of an engineer and the vigilance of a pilot, treating every inspection as if I were the one who would fly that aircraft next. I aim to be known not only for technical precision, but for understanding the underlying behaviors of aircraft systems and anticipating failures before they occur.

During my early career, I will immerse myself in inspection protocols, regulatory compliance, and complex troubleshooting. Long term, I intend to integrate my aviation maintenance credentials with my background in robotics engineering and computer science to launch ventures that improve local transportation infrastructure. I envision developing intelligent diagnostic tools, optimizing maintenance workflows, and contributing to the evolution of emerging aircraft technologies. My ambition is to advance not only my own career but also the safety, accessibility, and efficiency of the systems that communities depend on.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
’ve always been drawn to work that’s tangible — where you can see and feel the results of your effort. Growing up, I enjoyed working with my hands, fixing things, and figuring out how materials fit together. That naturally led me to welding. The first time I struck an arc and saw two separate pieces of metal fused into one solid structure, I knew this was something I wanted to master.
What inspired me to pursue welding as a trade was the combination of skill, precision, and creativity it requires. Welding isn’t just joining metal — it’s understanding heat, angles, materials, and structural integrity. There’s both science and artistry in laying down a clean bead. I was hooked by the challenge of improving my technique and the pride that comes from producing strong, high-quality work.
I’m drawn to hands-on work because it keeps me engaged and constantly learning. Every project is different — different metals, positions, blueprints, and environments. It requires focus, patience, and attention to detail. I appreciate that welding demands discipline and craftsmanship, and the results are something you can stand back and see at the end of the day.
There’s also a strong sense of responsibility in this trade. Whether it’s structural work, fabrication, or repair, people rely on welders to build things that are safe and built to last. Knowing my work contributes to something bigger — whether it’s infrastructure, equipment, or custom fabrication — gives me a real sense of purpose.
For me, welding isn’t just a job. It’s a skill I take pride in refining every day, and I’m motivated by the opportunity to keep improving and delivering work that’s strong, precise, and dependable.

My goal is to become a highly skilled, dependable welder who is trusted with structural and precision work. I want a career that offers both stability and growth. Welding provides the opportunity to work in a variety of industries, from construction and manufacturing to pipeline and custom fabrication. I plan to continue learning new techniques, improving my proficiency in different welding processes, and staying current with industry standards so I can remain competitive and versatile in the field.
This scholarship would play a significant role in helping me reach those goals. Financial support would reduce the burden of tuition, equipment, and certification costs, allowing me to focus fully on mastering my craft. My education is giving me more than technical skill — it is teaching me discipline, safety standards, blueprint reading, and problem-solving under real-world conditions. Those lessons will directly impact the quality of work I produce and the professional reputation I build.
With the combination of education, hands-on experience, and the support of this scholarship, I am confident I can establish a successful and meaningful career in welding. I am committed to working hard, continuing to improve, and contributing positively to the industry and my community.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
Growing up, skilled trades were not presented to girls as a career option or even for the fact that one day I would own a house and most likely need these skills. The message was always that girls belonged in healthcare, choose which healthcare field you want to go into. Healthcare will make you successful. I followed that path for a while, but it never felt right for me, and I don’t handle blood well! To say the least, I felt lost and unsure of what future I could build for myself.
I was inspired by watching homes in my neighborhood being remodeled and built.  I was dating a carpenter helping and getting the chance to help him with his projects. I loved learning and being capable of doing things I never once thought was imaginable for a girl. Remodeling houses really engaged my interests.  I thought I could have a career of this and never get bored. I searched tirelessly for opportunities in the trades industry for a position that would match my experience to get my foot in the door.
I started as a receptionist with a successful local home builder, then moved to become the owner’s executive assistant ad assistant to three other project managers. I learned the ins and outs of running a successful residential home builder company. Later I became an operations manager for another residential & commercial company, specializing in scaling and growing construction businesses.
Being in the office isn’t enough for me. I wanted to understand the field from the ground up, so I could be a better manager, make informed decisions and contribute more.  I went back to school to get a degree in construction management and an advanced certificate in Residential Construction. This education is giving me the opportunity to get hands on skills, strengthen my understanding of the trades and prepare me to inspire other young girls to see this industry as an obtainable and rewarding career path.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Looking ahead, I envision a fulfilling career where I can combine my operations experience with hands-on knowledge of the trades.   After graduation, I want to be a well-rounded construction manager who understands every aspect of residential construction from planning and coordinating in the office to problem solving on the job site. My goal is to grow within the industry, lead interesting projects, and mentor others, especially young girls, showing them that trades are not only accessible but empowering career paths. I hope to influence more girls to explore and enter the skilled trades. I plan on breaking down stereotypes that I faced growing up by encouraging young girls to pursue successful careers in the trades industry.
This scholarship and my education are essential to reaching my goals. By providing access to hands on training and industry recognized credentials, the program allows me to gain the skills I need to excel in the field and the office. It also supports my growth as a leader by strengthening my confidence and technical knowledge.
Ultimately, I hope to help new contractors structure their business and lead them to success in doing that I hope to make an impact to my state by bringing more housing to my community. Skilled trades are essential for building strong, vibrant neighborhoods and being part of that process motivates me every day. With the guidance, training and support this scholarship provides, I will be prepared to contribute fully to my field.
Activities/Extracurriculars: I take part in HBA events as well as ABC Greater Michigan events.  On 2/25/26 I put a booth on at the Build Like A Girl event from ABC GMCA. I set up a booth to represent Delta College. This event is to promote young girls to come and experience and learn about the trades industry.  The Booth was a station with drills, impacts and a few other power tools.  The girls got to practice using impacts to drill screws into 4x8 planks of wood.  I wanted the girls to experience holding a power tool and get excited to do something they may have never got to do before. Using power tools can be intimidating. Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
My path to the trades has been anything but traditional. For the past sixteen years, I've built a successful career in technology, working as a software developer, leader, coach, and international speaker, helping organizations improve how teams collaborate and innovate. While the work has been meaningful, it’s largely intangible; ideas, frameworks, and strategies - valuable, but often fleeting. Over time, I found myself drawn back to something I first experienced in a high school wood shop: the satisfaction of creating something real with my hands.

What began as home renovation projects and woodworking as a hobby, quickly became a deeper calling. Working with wood reintroduced me to a kind of creativity I'd been missing - one grounded in patience, craftsmanship, and creation. Using tools to shape raw material into something functional and lasting allows me to bring something entirely new into the world, transforming imagination into reality. That process feels deeply meaningful and has reshaped how I think about work.

Choosing woodworking and carpentry as a career represents an intentional shift toward work that is both tangible and enduring. This trade allowing me to combine design, construction, and entrepreneurship to build custom pieces and spaces that bring joy and storytelling into people’s homes. More importantly, it allows me to model a different path for others. Many adults feel trapped in careers that no longer inspire them, believing change is unrealistic. By transitioning from a corporate technology career into the skilled trades, I hope to demonstrate that change is possible at any stage of life.

I want my work to do more than produce furniture; I want it to reconnect people with the creativity and joy that comes with building. As someone who has spent years mentoring teams and students, I see skilled trades as a powerful way to strengthen communities by restoring the appreciation for craftsmanship and hands-on problem solving. Through my future woodworking business and continued mentorship, I aim to inspire others to rediscover their creative potential and to view skilled trades not only as viable careers, but as meaningful ways to contribute to the world around them.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
After graduating from the Sam Beauford Woodworking Institute in 2027, I envision building a career that blends craftsmanship, design, and entrepreneurship. My immediate goal is to launch and grow a custom woodworking and carpentry business focused on creating functional pieces and immersive spaces that combine high-quality construction with creativity and storytelling. I want my work to range from custom furniture to unique built-in projects that bring personality, joy, and lasting craftsmanship into people’s homes.

In the early years following graduation, my focus will be on refining my technical skills and brand through local client projects. Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I grew up in my dad's woodshop, watching him turn raw wood into something strong and beautiful with his calloused hands. He taught me to hold the tool right, always put safety first, and take real pride in my work. Those lessons stuck. As Alzheimer's slowly takes more of him, I can still hear his voice guiding me, I hold onto that wisdom tighter than ever.
For 25 years, my husband and I built a good life together. Running sales teams, owned an auto body shop, we both became personal trainers, weight-loss coaching while competing as a bodybuilder. I refinished old furniture, giving forgotten pieces new life. We raised our 5 kids, laughed together, felt like we had it all figured out.
COVID took our businesses overnight and almost took my dad. Diagnosed with Alzheimer's, he then got COVID and fought for his life in the hospital for over a month. We almost lost him.
We struggled for a long time after 2020. I would lie awake with a heavy heart, wondering how to keep the roof over our heads and hide the worry from our kids. We were often just days from complete ruin.
A family member suggested that I should look into Aviation Maintenance. I prayed, talked it over with my husband, and we did a walk-through of the school with the kids. It felt right. Going back to class after decades away, felt out of place at first. My teachers were kind and patient. Every breakthrough felt huge. I thought of my kids and dad every day. I wanted them to see that you keep going, even when life gets heavy.
Friday, I graduated from the Aviation Institute of Maintenance with my A&P license. My dad, after over a month in the hospital, got out just days before, in time to watch me walk across that stage. Seeing him there will mean more than words can say.
I am grateful for Dad's lessons, my husband's support, our kids who make everything worth it, and teachers who believed in a tired mom who would not quit.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
I see a life that finally feels steady and full of purpose again. After graduation, I plan to start my career as an aircraft mechanic in general aviation, working on small planes where the work is hands-on and every day brings new challenges. I want to be the reliable one in the shop, the person pilots and owners trust to get it right, because I know how much safety matters. I want come home each day feeling proud of what I accomplished.
With steady income, we can move out of this rundown rental into a safer, healthier mold free house preferably single-story home where the kids have space to grow and my husband can rest easier with his pain. We can get a second reliable car so we're not one breakdown away from missing appointments or school events. My 5 kids will see their mom succeeding, not just surviving, and that example means everything to me. I want them to know hard work and perseverance pays off, just like my dad taught me.
This education has already changed me. Going back to school at 48, keeping my grades high, earning the Top Wrench Award twice, it proved I can learn new things and rise to challenges. My A&P license opens the door to a skilled trade I love, one that honors my dad's lessons about building strong and safe.
A scholarship would make those goals more attainable a lot quicker. It would help pay down my school loans so massive payments don't eat into my starting paycheck. Instead of starting behind, I could focus on the job, saving for our future, and building the stability we've missed for years. It would remove one heavy burden and let me cross the finish line stronger.
I'm so close now. With my dad's pride in the audience at my graduation, my husband's support every step of the way, and my kids cheering me on, I feel ready. This scholarship and my education will help turn survival into thriving, for all of us. My heart is full of hope for what comes next.
Activities/Extracurriculars:
While I haven't had the chance to take on a lot of formal volunteer roles or extra-curricular activities outside of school these past couple of years, between full-time classes, caring for my dad with Alzheimer's, helping my mom around the house, and keeping up with my five kids at home.
At the Aviation Institute of Maintenance, one thing my teachers have always pointed out is my willingness to help with cleanup of our workstations and the shop areas after class or labs. I never leave a bay messy. I wipe down tools, put everything back where it belongs, sweep up metal shavings or fluid spills, and make sure the space is ready for the next group. I take real pride in a clean hangar. Coming from 25 years as a business owner, I know how much presentation matters. A clean, organized shop isn't just about looks; it shows respect for the equipment, for safety, and for the people who come in after you. In aviation, where precision and attention to detail can literally mean life or death, that habit feels even more important. My instructors have noticed it, and a couple have even mentioned it when talking about the Top Wrench Award. They said it was part of what set me apart, that consistent care for the workspace and the tools.
Beyond school, my extra time has mostly gone to family, but I've carried that same mindset into helping my mom with home repairs and renovations while my dad's Alzheimer's has made it harder for him to do those things himself. I've fixed leaky faucets, patched walls, painted rooms, and organized the garage the way my dad always kept tools in their places, everything labeled. It's not volunteer work in the traditional sense, but it's me stepping up to keep things running smoothly for the people who need it most, just like I do in the shop.
I also stay involved in small ways at school by mentoring or helping newer students when I can. If someone is struggling with a wiring diagram or torque specs during a lab, I explain it the way my dad used to teach me in his woodshop, patiently, step by step, no judgment. I remember how intimidating it felt walking into that first class at 48, feeling like the odd one out, and I want others to feel supported. It's not an official club or position, but it's something I do because I believe in lifting each other up in this field.
Looking ahead, once I'm working as a mechanic and things settle a bit more at home, I want to get more involved in aviation-related volunteering. Maybe helping with youth programs at local airports, like introducing kids to aviation maintenance, or assisting with community fly-ins and aircraft restoration projects. I love the idea of passing on what I've learned, just like my dad did for so many students over his 35 years of teaching woodshop and drafting. For now, though, my biggest "extra-curricular" has been showing up every day with that same pride in doing things right, cleaning up, helping classmates, and treating the shop like it's my own. It may not look flashy on paper, but it's how I've stayed committed to aviation and to the values that got me here.
I'm happy knowing these small habits are building toward something bigger.
