Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion: My background in the piano industry is rooted in a decade of practical experience. For the past ten years, I have balanced work in a university music environment with running my own independent tuning business. This dual role has taught me that the piano is a high-use machine of over 9,000 parts, and my experience as a trained player gives me a unique advantage in diagnosing exactly where a mechanism is failing to respond to the player's touch. What draws me to hands-on work is the precision required to bridge the gap between a machine's parts and its output. While my business has allowed me to develop a solid foundation in tuning and basic field repairs, I am pursuing formal training at North Bennet Street School to gain the advanced technical skills I currently lack: comprehensive regulation and voicing. These are the most demanding aspects of the trade, requiring adjustments measured in thousandths of an inch to ensure the action responds consistently across all eighty-eight keys. I am motivated by the physical "micro-adjustments" of the trade—where moving a capstan or shaping a hammer felt by a fraction of a millimeter changes the entire mechanical response. I prefer the workbench to the stage because it offers a direct, physical objective: diagnosing a mechanical failure and executing a solution. My goal is to scale my business to fill a critical gap in the industry. There is a high demand for technicians who can perform high-level regulation to keep existing instruments in peak condition. I want to spend my professional life ensuring these complex machines function at their highest capacity. For me, the appeal of the skilled trades lies in the manual labor and technical discipline required to maintain the mechanical integrity of the instrument.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship: Following my graduation from North Bennet Street School, my immediate career goal is to secure a position as a university technician or a concert tuner for a major symphony orchestra. These roles represent the pinnacle of the trade, requiring the exact high-level skills in regulation and voicing that the NBSS curriculum provides. Maintaining a fleet of instruments for a university or a professional orchestra is a massive mechanical responsibility; it requires ensuring that every piano can withstand the rigors of constant performance while meeting the exacting standards of world-class artists. Beyond my personal technical goals, I envision my career as a platform for advocacy within the skilled trades. I plan to host clinics and travel to universities to lecture on the piano technology field. My goal is to serve as a voice for the younger generation, demonstrating that music trades are fulfilling, financially viable, and rewarding careers. Many young people overlook the technical "backstage" roles that are essential to the arts. By sharing my journey from a player to a technician, I want to show that one can have a profound impact on the musical world through mechanical mastery and craftsmanship. This scholarship and my education at NBSS are the essential bridges to this future. The rigorous, master-apprentice training at NBSS provides the technical credibility and professional network required to enter the elite world of concert services. Furthermore, the financial support allows me to focus entirely on mastering these specialized skills without the burden of prohibitive debt. This education isn't just an investment in my own workshop; it is an investment in the next generation of the trade. By supporting my training, this scholarship enables me to become a master technician who not only preserves the mechanical integrity of our finest instruments but also inspires the next wave of tradespeople to keep this essential craft alive.
Additional Financial Details: The decision to pursue a career in piano technology at this stage of my life is born from a significant professional transition. For years, I believed my career path was set as a brass technician, but the sudden loss of that position last March forced a total reevaluation of my future. By becoming a high-level piano technician, I can secure my financial future while still participating in the orchestral world as a musician, without the "make-or-break" pressure of every audition. Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I've been intrigued by cars forever but never acted on it. As a woman who enjoys male-dominated things like video games, sports, and cars, it's been a bit hard trying to make sense of it all without the constant sexism and negativity toward women in these hobbies.
However, after my sister sat me down to watch the Canadian Grand Prix, my friend forced me to help work on his Jeep (because of my strange ability to find all the tools he needed in his garage), and my bestie dragged me out to car meets, it clicked. I fell in love, and I fell hard. I finally acted on my weird interest in cars and applied to my local tech school, and I'm so glad I did.
Since then, I haven’t missed a Grand Prix. I even went to the Miami 2025 race for my birthday on race day, and being there in person was amazing. Although it wasn't the main attraction, seeing the F1 Academy girls was life changing. Watching women change the rules in the motorsport world means everything to me.
In the future, I want to be part of that niche group of women in motorsport. That’s the big, crazy dream. I hope to make it there and take every opportunity to learn and grow in the car world.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Looking into the future has always been a challenge for me. Growing up as a military brat and moving constantly made it difficult to imagine where I would be even five years ahead. Now that my father has retired, I finally have the stability and space to picture what my future could look like.
After completing my two years at OTC, I plan to continue my education at UNOH. Their extensive automotive and motorsports programs align perfectly with my goals and would give me a strong jump start in the direction I want to pursue.
Beyond that, my long-term plan is a bit adventurous and flexible something that comes naturally to me after a lifetime of adapting to new environments. My dream is to work in motorsports. The biggest goal I hold is to become an engineer for Ferrari or any Formula team. I especially look up to the women in the Formula One Academy; I admire their passion, resilience, and determination to continue in motorsport despite the obstacles they face. I would love not only to join them in the industry but also to support them and contribute to their success.
While that is my ultimate dream, I also recognize the value of achievable steps along the way. Working in any American-based motorsport or even with local teams would be an incredible opportunity. I simply love building cars and solving problems on the fly. The idea of adjusting, improving, and helping athletes perform at their best is exciting and deeply fulfilling to me.
I’ve already started researching, networking, and learning more about the field. I know breaking into the automotive and motorsport world will take time and persistence, but I’m ready for that challenge. I’m eager to see where this journey takes me, and I’m committed to working hard, adapting as needed, and persevering until I make it possible.
Additional Financial Details:
During my first semester, I was fortunate to receive the Ford Scholarship, which covered most of my expenses. I am extremely grateful for that opportunity. I have remained in contact with them and have even discussed the possibility of a job or apprenticeship. Although those plans are still developing, I continue to reach out to the supportive women who helped me initially, in hopes of finding opportunities within the sports realm or other meaningful areas where I can grow.
This semester, financial stability has been a major challenge. I have been working to resolve my VA benefits, but due to complications—likely from my information being mixed with my sister's—I have not yet received any assistance. I recently started a new job to help support myself, but because of paperwork issues, I haven't received a reliable paycheck for almost a month. With inconsistent income and no benefits coming in, affording basic needs has been extremely stressful, especially while balancing school.
Transportation has added to the strain as well. My 2002 Honda Accord is currently inoperable after a windshield replacement attempt revealed rust, and the company declined to finish the repair. Without a functioning car, getting to school has been complicated and time-consuming, making this holiday season even more difficult to navigate.
With my previous scholarship funds fully used, VA payments unresolved, delayed paychecks, and the rising cost of living, any assistance or scholarship would significantly ease the financial burden I’m facing. It would allow me to focus more on my education and future rather than constant worry about how to stay afloat. Receiving this support would truly mean the world to me.
Activities/Extracurriculars:
Although I haven’t volunteered as much as I hope to in the long run, I’m eager to become more involved in my community—especially now that my father has retired from the military and my family is able to settle in one place. Moving frequently made it difficult to build long-term connections, but volunteering has always been something that brings me joy and purpose whenever I’ve had the opportunity.
One of my favorite experiences was volunteering at Four Paws for Ability in Ohio, where I helped desensitize and exercise service dogs in training. I spent time walking, playing with, and socializing them to prepare for their future roles with children who need service animals. Knowing I was contributing to a cause that directly improves lives was incredibly meaningful, and it strengthened my desire to stay involved with animal-focused organizations.
In Florida, I took an elective called The Buddy Program, where I volunteered in special education classrooms. I assisted teachers with grading, answering student questions, and reading aloud to help auditory learners. I formed genuine connections with many of the students, learned from them, and still check in with a few of them today. That experience taught me patience, empathy, and how rewarding it is to support others.
Now that I’m in a position to plant roots, I’m actively looking into local animal shelters and even a nearby bookstore where I hope to volunteer soon. Helping my community—whether that means supporting students, working with animals, or even rescuing a turtle crossing the road—has become something I value deeply. My goal is to continue growing my involvement and to use my time and skills to make a positive impact wherever I can.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
Well, first of all, I am diagnosed and treated for ADHD, and everything I read about ADHD being the best for skilled trades is true. I tried college a decade ago, before treatment. I had no clue what I wanted to do. I had no clear path, not even the hint of one. I skipped a lot of classes, dropped a lot of classes, failed a handful, had to write to keep my financial aid, and ultimately, I quit. Now, I am diagnosed and medicated to treat my ADHD, and I wish my parents hadn't gotten turned down previously, or tried a little harder, because what a change of direction I am in. But it was the 90s, and we lived in Alaska, so I can't blame them; they did try. 

I feel like I wasted a lot of time and got into a lot of debt from my previous schooling, and I used to feel like it was all a waste, but I did manage to get enough of the general education courses that will transfer to the technical college I am currently attending, and they will go towards my degree. When I graduate, I will have an Associate of Applied Science in Automotive Technology. That makes me feel like somebody.

My passion to work in this industry stems from my experiences. I do not know what took so long for me to realize it. For many, a vehicle is their lifeline. If they don't have a car, they can't get to work, they can't make money, they can't pay their bills, repeat. I personally have paid over $5,000 for guesswork to fix an emission light on a car that I did not own that was worth less than that, just to keep myself and my now wife going at that time. Somehow, fixing the emission light turned into jobs like getting CV axle boots.

I don't have enough word count to explain what inspired me... I just want to make a difference. I want to help people. I want honesty in the industry. I want to give back.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
After graduation, I envision finally having a better future for my family. My dad unexpectedly passed away in 2017. My wife and I moved in with my mom to help her out. I have been out of work since then. She has since partially retired from work, and she is starting to struggle a little bit. I want to take that struggle away. I know that Automotive Technology isn't the first thought to be able to do that, but I want to do something I know I love doing.

I know straight out of college that I will not be able to open my own shop, but that is my ultimate goal. Now that I am in school and know that getting an Associate's degree means I could teach automotive technology, that seems pretty interesting too. 

Until then, however, any scholarships I am awarded help tremendously in multiple ways. Not only to help pay for tuition, supplies, gas, and losses from being self-employed and taking time to go to school, but also any extra awarded amount could help me by building up my tools to get me going after I graduate. One of the things getting emphasis is "your toolbox" after graduating. Because "in the automotive industry, you do not want to be the one borrowing tools". 

At the technical college I am attending, students are given a 50% discount for Snap-On tools until the program ends. If I am awarded any scholarships, with any extra amount that I can afford, I plan to start building my toolbox. I do not want to go crazy and do want to think methodically about what purchases I will make if given the opportunity to do so. This is a 5-semester program, so it is going to go by pretty quickly, so I hope to take advantage of that opportunity as much as possible. 

I fall under the maximum FAFSA student aid index of -1500. My wife is going to be taking a bit of a loss of income due to my going to school. This scholarship would help in so many ways.
Additional Financial Details:
Because I have already gone to school previously, I have used up a little more than half of my Pell Grant. I did not know this was something that had a maximum percentage allocated. The job demanded accountability because any oversight could impact safety, reliability, and production. That environment shaped my work ethic and strengthened my diagnostic and mechanical skills.

My decision to transition into aviation mechanics comes from a desire to apply those same technical strengths in an industry where precision and safety are even more critical. Aviation represents the highest standard of maintenance discipline. Every inspection, torque value, and logbook entry carries weight. I am motivated by the responsibility that comes with maintaining aircraft, knowing that the quality of my work directly affects passenger and crew safety. The structured regulatory environment and emphasis on procedural compliance appeal to me because they align with my experience in safety-critical operations.

What draws me to hands-on work is the direct connection between problem-solving and results. I enjoy analyzing a malfunction, isolating the root cause, and restoring equipment to full operational condition. Working with tools, measuring tolerances, tracing electrical faults, and conducting operational checks provides a level of satisfaction that purely theoretical work cannot match. I appreciate seeing a system return to service because of careful troubleshooting and skilled execution.

I am also inspired by the engineering complexity of aircraft systems. The integration of structures, propulsion, avionics, hydraulics, and flight controls requires both technical knowledge and disciplined craftsmanship. My background in renewable energy provided a strong mechanical and electrical foundation, but aviation challenges me to refine those skills to a higher standard of precision.

Ultimately, I pursue skilled trades because they demand competence, continuous learning, and accountability. Aviation maintenance embodies those values, and I am committed to building a long-term career where technical excellence and safety remain the priority.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
Since I was 14, I have worked in the service industry doing work for minimum wage. Working these jobs has taught me some valuable lessons about life, but I currently have 3 jobs to make ends meet and am in need of change. I feel a strong desire to find a career that will allow me to take those trademark steps into adulthood like buying a house and starting a family.  
Having a more feasible work life balance is a strong motivator for pursuing a better career, but fear is even stronger. The current political climate in our country is growing more unstable by the day. Although I live in the safe bubble of Colorado, that doesn't mean that safety will last forever. As a trans Hispanic man (and a US citizen), I fear for myself and my family every single day. The ongoing threat of being ripped from your car and beaten for no reason is imminent. The threat of being rounded up and put in a camp for being a "nihilistic violent extremist" simply by existing is imminent. I am pursuing this career so that I can have a means of escape that can financially support my family anywhere we go.
All in all, I'm pursuing this career for personal growth of course, but it's no longer only about me. I make my choices in life for 2 now, and maybe later, more. I never thought I would be a textbook "family man", but finding the right person really changes everything. They will be working 4 jobs to pay most of the rent while I'm in school, which is why I am applying for this scholarship. I want to give them that work life balance that I'm chasing by pursuing this career, and so that I don't have to work myself to the bone having multiple jobs outside of full-time schooling as well. My classes may be paid for, but the never-ending stressors of life still go on outside of the classroom.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
I am inspired by the visionaries at places like Universal Studios and Meow Wolf. Their success has shown that art galleries, traditional plays, or movie studio tours will no longer satisfy the creative experience seeker. They want to feel transported into a story that has an intimate emotional connection and is far from their daily lives.
I am purposely choosing classes that will bring me closer to joining the building teams behind these projects; from prop fabrication to the steel framework that holds up theme park rides. In early February, I will begin a program that will teach the different welding techniques that are used in commercial fabrication. After gaining experience in the workforce, I plan to build my own immersive experience that can marry my passion for disability uplifting art and world building. I want to create a universe where the experience of those who are often an afterthought is put first.
My support for the disabled community began when I worked as an adaptive ski instructor. On my first shift, I helped a blind, partially deaf, autistic woman ski down the bunny hill and it changed me forever. She came in expecting to simply put the skis on and feel how they feel, but we said we can take it up a notch. So we took her, arms held by instructors, up the magic carpet lift and down the hill, and she was screaming with excitement all the way down. I left that day crying tears of pride in her accomplishment, because this company gives people experiences that they never thought they could have. There is so much power in that, and that is why I am determined to make an experience that creates the feeling of belonging for those who are denied it. I imagine doing this by making a deliberate choice to include things specifically for folks with various disabilities. I want to flip the script on the usual experience so that regularly abled people can feel how it feels to be excluded while also uplifting a community that could always use someone on their side.
Additional Financial Details:
My financial need is mostly related to housing and living expenses and to welding supplies needed for schooling. During my time in school, I will have to leave my job because my normal hours directly interfere with my in-class time. This leaves my partner to pay most of our rent, which means they will be working 4 jobs. I will have to take on a job to pull at least some of the weight financially because asking them to take on everything is far too much to ask of any person. I also have spent at least $500 on welding supplies to prepare for school which has put a significant dent in my savings which wasn’t very much to begin with. I would have used that money to pay for repairs on my car but unfortunately I no longer can afford it. This will render my car unsafe to drive until it is repaired, which will mean I will either have to ride a bike, ask for a ride, or use public transit. I normally wouldn’t mind using public transit, but about a year ago I witnessed a shooting while in a bus terminal and it has traumatized me to the point of not wanting to use public transit unless absolutely necessary. On top of the cost of rent, there are many other bills such as health insurance, car insurance, utilities, and so on that significantly increase my monthly payments.
Getting this scholarship would not only help me, but would be helping my family as a whole be able to pay for our housing without breaking our spirits. It would mean the absolute world to me to not have such stress looming over us every day and be able to be fully present in my studies so I can provide a better future for my family.
Activities/Extracurriculars:
My extracurricular activities in high school and college were mostly related to playing percussion. I was in marching band all throughout high school and was in concert band in high school and college. My entire life revolved around band for so many years because performing through music gave me a strength and confidence I couldn't find anywhere else. My marching band won nearly every championship we attended, with the percussion section winning in its category every time. In college, I also participated in Students for Justice in Palestine which was a club at NAU that met to educate those who weren’t aware of the history of the Middle East conflict and also to organize actions and fundraisers to support Palestinians. 
I also volunteered quite a bit in my earlier 20s. My time was given to underserved communities such as the trans community in CO and immigrants coming to the US in AZ. In Durango, CO, I worked as a co-facilitator for a discussion group called Tea Time which helped transgender youth work through topics such as transitioning, social representation, personal feelings, and growing up. It was so important to be a part of this group for me because I was also transitioning at the time, and the kids got to hear straight from me what it felt like and what steps I took to get where I was. I also spent time in Tuscon, AZ where I worked with an organization that helped immigrants who just left ICE detention to find their footing and get clothing and food for their families. My role was to sort clothing in the warehouse and search for certain items when needed.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
Those of us who are mechanically inclined are often drawn to anything under the sun that means we get to build or fix things. Discovering I was that kind of person felt like watching Tinker Bell in the beginning, where the fairies walk to the different podiums until one of them glows. I was also chosen to be a tinker. Being interested in mechanics was strange when cars were never even something that crossed my mind. I had my eyes in the sky. Maybe it was seeing ads on the TV of people in hangars that gave me the idea in the first place, but once it was in my head, I couldn’t get it out. I was from the middle of nowhere, lower-class and homeschooled. There were not opportunities to explore that interest, and yet it persisted. 
My grandfather was also a huge influence. He was in the Navy, and he got the same certification I’m conquering now when he was drafted in Vietnam. He worked on helicopters, specifically, and he claimed they were called “Aviation Mechanist’s Mates” rather than Aviation Maintenance Technicians. He never got the chance to talk about it much, as very few other people knew much about aircraft, let alone were licensed to operate and work on them (he was also licensed to fly helicopters). The moment I started going to that school, I remember saying, “Yeah, there’s this weird thing about helicopters…” and he said, “Oh, you mean how it makes NO sense that they’re able to fly?” 
I remember laughing because somebody finally understood my jokes. From there, it was like people were taking off invisibility cloaks one by one. Aviation people were everywhere, but nobody outside of their interest knew much about them. 
My desire to do hands on work used to come from necessity. I had ADHD with no treatment and was incapable of comprehending anything just by looking at it or thinking about it. Now I'm capable of doing that, but I am always happiest when I am moving around, working through it physically rather than hypothetically.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
Electrical work has always fascinated me because it is the backbone of how a home or building functions. Behind every switch and system is something essential that keeps life moving. Since I was young, I have been curious about how things work mechanically — not just using them, but understanding what makes them operate.
I grew up on the mountainside of Peru as the youngest of eight siblings. Watching my older brothers take apart machines, repair cars, and rewire systems inspired me to learn with my hands and see challenges as opportunities to build something better. Their work ethic and determination showed me that skill comes from patience, curiosity, and persistence.

Inspired by them, I pursued electrical studies at a university in Peru, where I completed two years of education before moving to the United States. Although my studies were cut short, my drive to learn never stopped. Since arriving in America, I have worked in construction and gained hands-on experience across many trades, including electrical, plumbing, drywall, flooring, installations, and repairs. Through years of dedication, I built the confidence to begin working for myself and take pride in completing projects above expectations.

Life has not been easy, and I have faced many hardships to get to where I am today. However, every challenge has shaped me, strengthened my perseverance, and pushed me to keep moving forward toward my goals.

Now, I am working toward earning my electrician license with the dream of starting my own business and serving residential, commercial, and industrial communities. Growing up in Peru taught me the importance of community, and I want my work to reflect that same spirit of support and reliability. This scholarship would help me continue my training and turn years of hard work into a licensed profession built on skill, integrity, and service.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Just as electrical systems quietly power the spaces people depend on every day, I want my future career to help power strong and reliable communities. After graduation, my first goal is to become a licensed electrician and continue developing my technical skills through hands-on experience. Electrical work is more than a career path for me; it is a way to build safe, reliable environments for families, businesses, and entire communities. I want to be known as someone people can depend on when they need quality work done with integrity and pride.

As I continue building my career, my long-term vision is to start my own electrical and skilled trades business. I want my company to serve residential, commercial, and industrial communities with dependable, high-quality work while also creating opportunities for others who want to build careers with their hands. Throughout my life, from watching my brothers work on machines growing up in Peru to gaining experience across multiple trades in construction, I have seen how powerful mentorship and hands-on learning can be. Through my business, I want to encourage others to pursue electricity and the skilled trades by offering guidance, stable jobs, and opportunities to grow professionally.

My skilled trades education will give me the technical knowledge, safety training, and professional confidence to turn this vision into reality. The SkillPointe Foundation Skilled Trades Scholarship will help power me toward these goals by allowing me to continue my education, complete my licensing process, and focus on developing my skills without financial barriers holding me back.

Ultimately, I want my work to do more than complete projects — I want it to create opportunities, strengthen communities, and inspire others to see the skilled trades as a path toward stability, purpose, and long-term success.
Additional Financial Details: In addition to pursuing my education, I help support both of my parents who live here in the United States while continuing my full-time daytime work serving my clients and community through my own business. Because I am self-employed, I do not have dental, vision, health, or other personal insurance, and neither do my parents, which adds financial pressure as I continue my skilled trades training. My girlfriend and I are currently living with her parents because we are not yet financially able to afford a home, get married, and start a family while I continue building my career and education. These challenges have strengthened my determination and work ethic, and they continue to motivate me to build a stable and secure future. Receiving this scholarship would help relieve financial strain and allow me to stay focused on completing my training, earning my electrician license, and creating long-term stability for my family while continuing to serve my community through the skilled trades.
Activities/Extracurriculars: Helping others has always been important to me. I assist friends, neighbors, and local families with home repairs and projects, especially when they don’t have the resources to do it themselves. I also teach others how to work in the skilled trades on real job sites, sharing knowledge about tools, safety, and hands-on problem-solving. Beyond construction, I help people fix mechanical issues with cars, small engines, and other machines, showing them that challenges can be solved with guidance and persistence. Giving back and sharing my skills has always been one of the most rewarding parts of my life.

I was fascinated by how things worked and how to make them better. Until high school, my life followed a familiar routine with the same school and friends. Transferring schools my sophomore year disrupted that routine and challenged me, especially since I’ve never been very outgoing.  While choosing classes, I noticed a welding course on my schedule. At first, I saw it as a break from core classes, but once I started, I fell in love with it. Welding gave me a renewed sense of purpose and structure. Under the hood, nothing else mattered except the weld in front of me. Through hard work, I stood out in class and naturally stepped into a leadership role, helping others improve their skills.  Now, in a fabrication class, I’ve led multiple projects. While I’ve always earned good grades, the classroom never excited me. Welding does—it lets me work with my hands and do something I truly love.

I now hold two industry-recognized qualifications.  I serve as my local chapter’s SkillsUSA representative through Great Oaks.  I recently was selected to participate in the Southwest Regional SkillsUSA competition.  Always striving for more has driven me to further my knowledge at Hobart. I was also fortunate to have upperclassmen who were able to mentor me, which I think is very important in the trades.  I would love to further my knowledge to later pass it on to the next generation.

I have been accepted to attend the Hobart Institute of Welding, beginning in September 2026, where I am enrolled in the Pathways Welding Program. This is a 40-week program. The training I will receive directly supports my future career goals as a welder and fabricator. This hands-on training will prepare me to enter the workforce with the technical ability, work ethic, and confidence needed to build a successful career in welding.

With an aerospace company in my hometown, I plan to pursue a position there after completing my education. I believe this clear career path will lead to a successful future.

My ultimate goal in the industry is to create an honest, hard working career for myself.  My future goal is to own nice things and provide for my family. Sgt. Bertram Memorial 5K-9 Race/Walk
2020 – Present

That race is called the Sgt. Bertram Memorial 5K-9 Race/Walk. The 5K-9 race is held in remembrance of my uncle, fallen K-9 Officer Sgt. Benton Bertram, who lost his life bravely serving his community on Dec. 12th, 2018. Uncle Ben’s love of dogs inspired this event to be dog friendly and encourages participants to bring their furry friends to run the race! That’s why it’s called a 5K-9. My family has proudly organized this race since 2019. I have been there every step of the way! This spans the months of prep work to the 5am set-up on race day. 
The funds raised are split between two beneficiaries. The 550 Foundation, a 501(c)(3) non-profit organization established in Uncle Ben’s honor, provides free, fully trained K-9s, training, and equipment to law enforcement K-9 divisions throughout Indiana. Then different local children are chosen each year that are facing a major life event. This ranges from severe illness to tragic loss of parents. I have had the opportunity to meet these young people every year. 
When I’m asked to describe how this volunteer experience has made a difference in the lives of others. The obvious answer would be to talk about the amazing amount of support that the funds we have raised over the years have helped ease the burden of the hardship that the child is facing. It would be easy to speak of the incredible rush I feel when I get to stand with my family and take a picture with the child and their family, while we hand over a huge check. Or the times when we get to meet the new K-9 dog that our funds help to pay for through the 550 Foundation. And then, to hear of the big drug bust that our donated K-9 dog helped to facilitate. But that would be skipping over the most important part, the healing that I have seen within my family through our participation. 
I love it when my mom explains that we have turned our pain into purpose. I have seen the smiles return to the faces of my family members while embracing the beneficiaries, race volunteers, sponsors, and dedicated racers that show up every single year. This race is more than a charity event and volunteer opportunity. This race is a community healing event that brings everyone together that loved my Uncle Ben, while at the same time, supporting others in the community that need our help. 
It has been my absolute privilege to be involved in such a powerful community event and witness firsthand what it feels like to simultaneously help others while being healed myself. I now get to carry these lessons into my adult life and share my story in hopes of helping others.
Due to my family being the organizers for this event, I have a large behind the scenes role. These duties are as follows: Pre-race consists of preparing Sponsorship Packets and gifts, pick- up/sort/assemble dog toys/treats/grooming supplies for Top Dog prizes, stuff 150+ letters to deliver to all residents along race route, pack race packets with racer shirt/race bib/pins, organize 400+ race packets into alphabetical order to prepare for packet pick-up. 
Day of race: race set-up begins at 5:30am, setting up traffic cones, unpacking 25 cases of bottled water and putting on ice for racers, assisting in packet pick-up area handing out race packets, acting as race ambassador and greeting racers when they arrive and assisting them with their questions. Assist my family if any last-minute errands need to be run before the race starts.
Post race: clean-up 

Christmas with a Cop – Charlestown Police Department
2018 - 2023

Looking back, many of my 2nd Wednesdays in December started with the crisp air of a parking lot and the bright, swirling lights of patrol cars. As the child of a police officer, I began participating in the "Christmas with a Cop" program long before I was old enough to understand the logistics of a budget or the complexities of social need. Back then, I was just a tag-along helper, watching my father trade his "tough cop" persona for a smile as wide as the toy aisle.
As I transitioned from a young child to a high schooler, my role in the program evolved, and so did my understanding of its impact. What once felt like a fun day of holiday shopping became a profound lesson in empathy. I moved from simply pointing out the "coolest" toys to helping children navigate the difficult choices of picking out warm coats and sturdy boots, items they often prioritized over the toys they clearly desired.
Working alongside my dad has been the highlight of this experience. Seeing him interact with the community in such a gentle, humanizing way showed me a side of law enforcement that I deeply admire. We weren't just "shopping"; we were building a bridge of trust and providing a moment of pure, unburdened joy for families facing incredible hardships.
The most transformative part of this journey has been the shift in my own heart. It is easy to take a warm home and a full tree for granted until you stand in a checkout line with a child who is genuinely moved to tears by a new pair of shoes. Every year, I leave the store feeling a "holiday high" that no physical gift could ever provide.
Participating in Christmas with a Cop has been a family tradition. It taught me that the true spirit of the season isn't found in what you receive, but in the perspective you gain. I enter the store to help others, but I always leave feeling like the lucky one, deeply appreciative of the life I have and the father who taught me how to share it.

My father’s drug addiction and lack of responsibility led to the divorce, years of absence from our lives, and even the loss of our furniture and belongings as he sold them to support his addiction.
The aftermath deeply affected my mother. Throughout my childhood, she struggled with narcotic abuse and alcoholism. Her instability shaped my siblings’ and my upbringing: we moved eight times, spent a summer living with our grandparents because we had no stable home, and constantly worried about whether our mother was safe. These circumstances forced me to grow up quickly, manage shifting environments, and learn how to advocate for myself and my family.
A turning point came when my brother and I received scholarships to attend private Catholic high school. Each day, we travel 35 minutes each way, leaving home at 7:30 am and returning around 8:30 or 9:00 pm after sports and activities. The long days are challenging, but they symbolize my commitment to using education as a path toward stability and a different future. At school, I have pushed myself academically while also seeking out teachers, counselors, and coaches who could guide me. I have taken on responsibilities in sports activities, and I try to support classmates who are dealing with their own challenges, because I understand how much it matters to feel seen and encouraged.
Despite everything, my siblings and I never lost each other. As I grew older, I began to see the quiet strength in my older brothers and sister, who more closely remember the chaos surrounding the divorce. Watching them confront their own pain, work jobs, and still pursue college showed me what resilience truly looks like. Their determination taught me how to be brave, how to break destructive cycles, and how to believe that higher education is possible even when the odds seem stacked against you.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I am from Longview, Texas, and I attended school in Hallsville, Texas while living in a low-income, single-parent household. Despite limited resources, I had an unrelenting passion for cars. By the age of six, I had grown an extensive Hot Wheels collection that spans the entirety of my home bedroom. This gave me the ability to identify most makes and models on the road at a young age. I had an undying interest in vehicles, and I loved to dissect mechanical and electrical contraptions to find out how they worked; consequently, I gained an interest in automotive technology. 
My fascination with cars quickly evolved into a job aspiration when I enrolled in my high school's automotive technician class. Initially unfamiliar with tools such as ratchets and torque wrenches, my skills grew exponentially under the guidance of my instructor, Mr. Barrett. Breaking down the mechanical fundamentals of each vehicle in the school shop reinforced my passion for not only vehicles, but in-depth, manual repair as well. I seized every opportunity to learn, joining the Hallsville FFA Tractor Technician team and later the Hallsville SkillsUSA Auto Tech team. By graduation, I had extensive, entry-level experience in automotive technology, ready to pursue my career.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
I look forward to having a positive future and successful career as an automotive technician, and the resources and connections provided by Dallas College will undoubtedly give me the ability to make my aspirations a reality. Funding from the Skillpointe Foundation Skilled Trades Scholarship will allow me to purchase necessary tools and equipment needed without financial strain, so I can focus on working and learning as an automotive technician.
Dallas College, specifically the Brookhaven campus, has been amazing so far, and it has provided extensive resources, connections, and in-depth instruction to provide the foundation of a fruitful automotive career. The campus' perfect blend of book instruction and hands-on demonstration is like no other, and every lesson leads to further anticipation for future applications. The skills passed down to me from the knowledgeable instructors at this institution will allow me to eventually live a comfortable life while constantly diagnosing, learning, and understanding the complex vehicles that fill the streets.
The Skillpointe Foundation Skilled Trades Scholarship is more than just funding for me, it is a pathway to a strong career. The unfortunate aspect of becoming a technician is the higher cost of entry. Education tuition and tools alone cost as high as tens of thousands of dollars. Dallas College as well as prospective employers require students to have a set of tools for each institution. This indicates that at least two, extensive tool sets are required for every student, not including a third set for personal projects. Furthermore, there are no dorms at Dallas College, so independent students need to pay rent for their own apartment while attending school. This scholarship will help me overcome a large portion of these financial burdens, allowing me to extensively direct attention to my automotive career goals.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I did not grow up thinking I would go into a skilled trade, but looking back it makes sense. I have always liked figuring out how things work. When something breaks, my first thought is not to throw it away. I want to take it apart and see what happened. I like using my hands and actually seeing the result of my work in front of me.

Right now I am working toward a career in HVAC. I chose this trade because it connects to the work I already do with ice vending machines. I can handle most of the mechanical and electrical parts, but refrigeration is the one area where I know I need more training. Instead of guessing, I decided to go to school and really learn it the right way. I want to understand superheat, subcooling, airflow, and system design so I can diagnose problems with confidence.

Hands on work draws me in because it feels real. When I fix something, I can see it working again. I can hear it turn on. I can feel the cold air blowing. That gives me a sense of accomplishment that sitting behind a desk never did. I also like that skilled trades are always needed. People will always need heating and cooling. It feels good knowing I am learning something useful that helps families stay comfortable and safe.

One challenge I have faced is balancing school, work, and financial responsibilities. Tools and tuition are not cheap, and I still have bills to pay. There are days when I am tired and would rather rest, but I remind myself why I started. I am doing this to build a better future and to become more skilled at what I do.

Choosing a trade is not the easy path, but it is the right one for me. I am proud to work with my hands, solve real problems, and keep learning every day.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
After graduation, I see myself working full time in the HVAC field and continuing to build my skills every year. My goal is not just to get a job, but to become someone people trust. I want to be the technician that can walk up to a system, figure out what is wrong, and fix it the right way the first time. I do not want to guess. I want to understand exactly why something failed and how to prevent it from happening again.

I also plan to use my HVAC education to grow my ice vending business. Right now I can handle most repairs, but refrigeration is the most important part of those machines. Once I graduate, I will be able to service and troubleshoot my own equipment with confidence. That will save money, reduce downtime, and help my business grow stronger. In the future, I would like to own multiple machines and possibly expand even more. Having this trade gives me security because I will always have a skill I can rely on.

This scholarship would make a big difference in helping me reach those goals. School is not just tuition. It is tools, books, gas, and time away from work. Financial stress can be a distraction. With support from this scholarship, I would be able to focus more on learning and less on worrying about how to cover the next expense. It would also remind me that others believe in what I am working toward.

Education is giving me knowledge, confidence, and opportunity. This scholarship would help strengthen that foundation so I can graduate prepared, skilled, and ready to build the future I have been working toward.
Additional Financial Details:
One thing that has really opened my eyes is the true cost of going into a skilled trade. I recently asked my instructor which tools he recommends so I can be fully prepared and not fall behind. He went through the list and circled the ones he believes are important to have. When I added everything up, it came out to about four thousand dollars in additional tools alone.

That is on top of the three thousand dollars I have already spent for my first term, and that does not even include the next two terms of tuition and required tools. Skilled trades are hands on, which means you cannot just show up with a notebook. You have to invest in your equipment so you can practice, learn correctly, and be ready for real job sites.

I am committed to finishing this program no matter what, but the financial side is definitely a challenge. I am balancing work, school, and business responsibilities while trying to keep up with these expenses. Any support would help take pressure off and allow me to focus more on becoming the best technician I can be.

Beyond representation, I am excited about the many new skills I will develop. Welding requires precision, technical knowledge, and creativity. Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
Growing up in Toledo, I was the kid who took everything apart. TVs, video games, stereos, if it had screws, I was opening it up. My mom used to get so upset, but I wasn’t trying to break anything. I just needed to know how it worked. Unfortunately, I couldn’t get it back together and that’s what would lead my curious mind into a punishment.

At 17, I joined the military and became an aviation machinist’s mate. I spent nine years on active duty working on aircraft, mostly engines. That experience shaped me. Military aviation teaches you quickly that details matter and shortcuts don’t exist. When people step onto an aircraft, they’re trusting someone like me with their lives.

After the military, I worked on aeroderivative gas turbine engines with FieldCore GE and later supported lunar testing operations at NASA’s facility in Houston as a contractor with  Jacobs Engineering. Those were incredible opportunities, and I learned a lot. But deep down, I missed working on planes. I missed the sound, the environment, the unpredictability and the responsibility that comes with aviation.

That’s what led me to pursue my A&P license. Not because I had to start over but because I wanted to grow. More than anything, I’m doing this for my two sons. I want them to see that growth never stops. Even after nine years in the military and years in the field, I’m still willing to learn, improve, and chase my childhood curiosity. I want them to understand that providing for your family is important  but so is going after what brings you joy.

This isn’t just about a license. It’s about showing my kids that you never stop chasing your passion.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion: I am 17-years-old. I have worked since I was 14-years old. There are several  reasons that I want to become an electrician. However, I am listing four reasons that inspired me most to work in a skilled trade. First, I want to become an electrician because I enjoy solving problems. When I work with my hands, I see puzzle pieces that fit together. I have a picture in my head about how a thing should work, and I can put the steps in order to make it work. Second, it’s the challenge that makes me want to become an electrician. It’s not an easy field to learn. Also, I can advance by continuing my training. Third, the job market supports the trades. There are electrician-related job openings across the country. The Bureau of Labour and Statistics states that there is growing demand for qualified electricians. Fourth, anyone who can create, install, or repair will be able to support himself. I was told that my great-great-grandfather, Coy Galyean, built an airplane in his garage. The propeller was sent to the Grayson County, Virginia, museum. Additionally, my great-grandfather, Jack Galyean, built and wired houses. He also helped his father run all of the overhead telephone wires for Baywood, Virginia. One day, he slipped and ripped open his leg on one of those pole foot pegs. Not to worry though, great-great-grandpa poured mercurochrome into the hole. Great-grandpa passed out, but the next day, he went right back up another pole. They worked in cold and hot weather for very little pay. They were self-trained because schools were nonexistent in their county. They experimented with electricity and wiring. I understand that they joked about getting shocked. Maybe, they didn’t know the real dangers and just got lucky. These people are what I’m made of, that stuff, a need to accept and roll over challenges. However, I don’t want to be “just lucky”. I want to be trained as a tradesman, an electrician. Someday, I want my great-great-grandchildren to say,”Look what he did…in outer space!  I can do that too.”
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship: After I graduate from the YTI Electrical Technology Program, I envision myself as being on the second step of a staircase. Next, I will need to be hired as an apprentice.This is the third step. I realize that I will be judged by electrician employers as just above entry level. However, due to my YTI education, I will have an advantage over other job seekers, who were not school trained. Additionally, if I volunteer for Habitat for Humanity, I can gain experience. In Pennsylvania, there is no state electrician license. Although, if you own an electrical contracting business, you must register with the Attorney General’s Office. However, there are city requirements. So, the fourth step is becoming licensed for the city in which I will work. You can find all of this information on staterequirements.com, by Kay Venturi’s, “How to become an Electrician in Pennsylvania, 2026.”You will discover that a person only needs a high school diploma or GED, in order to become an electrician. However, would you want your home to be wired by an uneducated electrician? Then, I must pass a National Electrical Code exam. Passing the exam will put me on the fifth step. The sixth step is to apply and pay for my city license. Of course, I may want to build my own business which will mean that I will need several city licenses. Step seven is continuing to update my training. The YTI Program assists graduates in updating their electrical program education. Step eight is to give back to my school. I want to help fund scholarships for other electrician students. Also, once they graduate, I want them to come to me for help when they get stuck on a problem. It’s never wrong to ask for help. Finally, my overall goal to train for something that I really want to do will be met by the YTI Electrical Technology Program. I will own a career that I enjoy because I wanted it, not because it was needed to survive. This training will help me achieve my goal.
Additional Financial Details: I feel bad that I am mentioning these circumstances because other people might be in a worse situation. However, I need the tuition so I’ll share. First, my mom and dad are both employed, but they are paying for my older brother and sister’s student loans. Second, they have the worst luck with their cars. They have been hit numerous times in the past year. Third, my father has an irregular heart beat so the hospital must restart his heart rhythm at least once per year. It’s painful. Normally, he goes back to work the next day. He works an hour away from home. My mom has reactive hypoglycemia and works the night shift. She looks tired all of the time. My sister is a nurse, but she moved back home to help pay off some of her debt. My brother moved out, but could take only one student loan with him. Then his car was totaled in an accident. Like I said, bad luck! To finish, I have a condition called, chiari malformation. There is no great impact now, but when I’m older, I would like to have an insurance plan that allows for Botox treatments to treat headaches. Tylenol is great, but Botox is longer lasting.  An electrician can afford insurance that pays for Botox.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:

That curiosity pushed me to pursue certifications in carpentry, electrical, plumbing, and construction management. Each class and hands-on project confirmed that I belonged in the trades.

What draws me most to hands-on work is transformation. Construction demands problem-solving, precision, teamwork, and leadership. Every project presents challenges, and I find fulfillment in overcoming them and seeing visible progress each day.

As a woman in the construction industry, I also understand the importance of representation. I want to help reshape perceptions of who belongs in the trades. My long-term goal is to develop eco-friendly, affordable housing and create opportunities for more women — especially Black women — to enter and succeed in this field. Skilled trades are not just a profession for me; they are a pathway to generational impact and community development.

This scholarship would support my continued education and strengthen my ability to lead, build, and inspire others through my work. I am committed to mastering my craft and using it to create lasting change.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
In 2012, my family moved to a small, tight-knit community that would soon become our family. After turning 17, and a few months into starting my sophomore year at Cook Inlet Academy, I was diagnosed with pediatric cancer. While the medical battles were mine to fight, the burden was carried by the neighbors, friends, and even strangers in this town who stepped up to support us in every way imaginable. Now, as I prepare to complete my treatment in March 2026, my goal is to transition from being the one helped to being the one who helps.
The problem I aspire to solve is the lack of accessible, dependable, and trustworthy technical support in rural communities. In small towns, a broken-down vehicle isn't just an inconvenience; it can mean a missed paycheck or an inability to get to medical appointments. Having relied on the generosity of others for over a decade, I understand the weight of uncertainty. I want to remove that weight for others by becoming a skilled mechanic.
By attending a local trade school, I am not just learning a job; I am gaining a toolset to serve. My vision is to establish a presence in this community as a mechanic who prioritizes integrity and reliability. I want to be the person the elderly neighbor calls when their engine won't turn over, or the person a struggling single parent can trust for an honest repair.
Furthermore, I want to contribute to a long-term solution by mentoring the next generation. I hope to teach young people in our area how to be mechanically inclined, fostering self-sufficiency and pride in manual labor.
My journey through cancer has taught me that a community is only as strong as the people willing to serve it. By mastering this trade, I will ensure that the community that has blessed me since 2012 receives a return on its investment—one repair and one mentored student at a time.

Essay 1 - Background & Driving Passion:
I am inspired to pursue an Occupational Therapy Assistant credential at this juncture of my role as owner of BeneFit Personal Training because I no longer want to simply help older adults exercise; I want to help them function. Every day, I work with adults 55+ who are strong enough to lift a weight but unsure how to safely get off the floor, transfer into a car, or cook without pain. I see the gap between rehabilitation and wellness, and I want to stand confidently in that space with clinical depth, not just fitness expertise.

My grandmother, Phoebe, taught me that independence means dignity. OTA training would equip me to protect that dignity, not only through strength training, but through safe mobility, adaptive strategies, and aging-in-place support. This credential would allow me to merge medical insight with movement, strengthening both my practice and the community I serve.

As Richmond’s aging population grows, our community needs providers who understand both movement science and functional occupation. By earning an OTA credential, I can help older adults not only move better, but live better, being able to cook longer, drive longer, and play with grandchildren longer. This step reflects my commitment to building a model of care that blends medical insight, exercise, and purpose-driven aging.
Essay 2 - Post-Graduation & Scholarship:
Being an OTA doesn’t replace my identity in the Metro Richmond area as a person-focused medical exercise specialist.  It deepens it.  It transforms my community image from “Excellent older adult fitness provider” into “Clinically grounded functional independence specialist.”
And that...that’s a powerful shift.

From a service perspective, BeneFit Personal Training could be known as “The transition partner after physical therapy ends.”

Imagine that after hospitals discharge older adults, PT covers acute recovery.

My company would step in for functional endurance, home safety, and sustained independence.

Our team could ensure that older adults could (1) get up off the floor, (2) cook safely, (3) transfer confidently and, more importantly, (4) NOT return to the ER from preventable falls.

BeneFit Personal Training now plays a powerful community role.
Additional Financial Details: This powerful gesture of scholarship financially allows me to re-career from my 15 years in human resources, without the emotional and financial toll of taking out additional student loans and adding the burden of building a community partnership company, while also incurring heavy debt to be paid off from funding that could be used to best serve RVA's Baby Boomers in life-affirming ways.
Activities/Extracurriculars: In addition to building my company, I have been an avid and active volunteer with Blessing Warriors RVA. A non-profit in the area that serves homeless and insecurely housed individuals in Metro Richmond, Virginia, 365/24/7. It has given my heart joy to support my fellow brothers and sisters in meaningful ways of serving food, making toiletry packages, donating to emergency hotel room stays, etc.
